---
title: ""
page-layout: full
---

::: {.project-header}
::: {.container}

[← Back to Research](../research.qmd){.back-link}

# Flood Risk & Hazard Mapping, Kerr County TX

::: {.project-meta}
<span><strong>Course:</strong> Advanced GIS & Remote Sensing</span>
<span><strong>Instructor:</strong> Prof. Morgan Levy</span>
<span><strong>Term:</strong> Winter 2026</span>
<span><strong>Tools:</strong> Google Earth Engine · CHIRPS · Sentinel-1 SAR · Dynamic World · QGIS</span>
:::

:::
:::

::: {.container style="max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; padding-bottom: 5rem;"}

## The Question

Can satellite observations help evaluate and update FEMA's outdated flood hazard maps? And what do those maps miss — specifically in a community that just experienced a catastrophic flood?

## Why It Matters

On July 4, 2025, a catastrophic flash flood struck Kerr County, Texas — killing 27+ people at Camp Mystic, many in structures that were **outside FEMA's mapped flood zones**. FEMA maps had not been substantially updated in decades. They were built on historical river hydrology data, with no account for rainfall intensity patterns, urban expansion, or climate change.

This is not a Kerr County problem. Approximately 75% of U.S. flood insurance maps are considered outdated. The binary SFHA zoning framework — you're either in the flood zone or you're not — leaves millions of at-risk properties uninsured and countless communities underprepared.

## Methodology

We used four satellite-derived datasets, all accessed via **Google Earth Engine**, to interrogate FEMA's existing maps from multiple angles:

| Dataset | Source | What it measures |
|---|---|---|
| CHIRPS precipitation | UCSB Climate Hazards Group | Daily rainfall estimates, 1981–present |
| Sentinel-1 SAR | ESA Copernicus | Flood extent via radar backscatter change |
| Dynamic World | Google/WRI | Land cover change, urban expansion |
| JRC GLOFAS | European Commission JRC | Modeled 100-year flood hazard zones |
| FEMA NFHL | FEMA | Regulatory Special Flood Hazard Areas |

**Key workflows:**
- Wrote JavaScript scripts in GEE to filter CHIRPS by date range and extract daily precipitation grids
- Ran Sentinel-1 pre/post-event backscatter change detection (June 29 → July 4, 2025) at 10m resolution
- Used Dynamic World to quantify urban expansion in Kerr County: ~13% built-area growth from 2016–2025
- Overlaid all layers with JRC 100-year modeled flood zones and FEMA SFHA boundaries

## Key Finding

::: {.project-visual}
*[Map: CHIRPS precipitation, July 3–7 2025 vs. JRC 100-year flood corridor — add your image here]*
::: {.visual-caption}
The heaviest precipitation during the July 2025 event fell **outside** the JRC-modeled flood hazard corridor — a structural gap between where rain falls and where regulatory risk is mapped.
:::
:::

::: {.project-visual}
*[Map: Sentinel-1 SAR flood extent vs. FEMA SFHA — add your image here]*
::: {.visual-caption}
Sentinel-1 backscatter change detection showing observed flood proxy (blue-green) extending significantly beyond FEMA's Special Flood Hazard Areas (magenta = outside SFHA).
:::
:::

::: {.project-visual}
*[Map: Dynamic World urban expansion 2016–2025 — add your image here]*
::: {.visual-caption}
~13% built-area growth in Kerr County from 2016–2025, primarily expanding outward from existing flood zones — increasing impervious surface runoff in areas not accounted for in current FIRMs.
:::
:::

## What I Found

Three structural gaps in FEMA's approach:

1. **Precipitation is not modeled.** FEMA maps predict where water goes after entering river channels — not where rainfall is heaviest. CHIRPS shows dramatic within-county variation that FEMA's maps can't capture.

2. **Urban expansion is invisible to static maps.** A 13% increase in built area over a decade changes runoff dynamics fundamentally. FEMA has no mechanism to update for this without a formal, expensive remapping process.

3. **Binary zoning creates false safety.** The SFHA/non-SFHA distinction communicates certainty where none exists. Many of the Camp Mystic structures were outside the SFHA — and therefore uninsured.

## Future Research Direction

The natural next step — which I identified in the project's future research section — is a **multi-criteria flood susceptibility index** combining precipitation intensity, topography (DEM), urban expansion, and regulatory boundaries. This is the same MCDA framework I applied to the Attica wildfire context, now translated to flash flood risk. That project is underway.

## Code & Files

[View on GitHub](https://github.com/javramis){.cta-secondary style="padding: 0.6rem 1.4rem; border: 1.5px solid #1a1a2e; color: #1a1a2e; border-radius: 2px; font-size: 0.85rem; letter-spacing: 0.08em; text-transform: uppercase; text-decoration: none; display: inline-block;"}

:::
